retrieved_docs.title: Acute Anemia -- Etiology -- Extracorpuscular hemolytic anemias
retrieved_docs.content: Complement-mediated thrombotic microangiopathy : This disorder  is defined by the abnormal activation of the complement system, resulting in the formation of small blood clots within the microvasculature. This phenomenon can manifest in various disorders, including atypical HUS, which also presents with a triad of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and renal failure, though renal involvement is more severe as compared to TTP. [10] [11] Disseminated intravascular coagulation : DIC is characterized by widespread systemic activation of coagulation throughout the body in response to various underlying causes, such as infections, severe trauma, or certain complications during pregnancy. DIC results in excessive formation of intravascular fibrin, causing thrombosis. However, as coagulation factors are consumed, bleeding complications can arise. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Pernicious Anemia -- Differential Diagnosis -- Other causes of megaloblastic anemia:
retrieved_docs.content: myelodysplastic syndromes hemolytic anemia thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Anemia -- Etiology -- Extracorpuscular hemolytic anemias
retrieved_docs.content: Mechanical destruction or m icroangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MAHA) : This  is a type of anemia characterized by the destruction of red blood cells in small blood vessels due to mechanical injury from microthrombi formation, often associated with conditions like Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP), hemolytic-uremic syndrome (HUS), or DIC. TTP is characterized by the formation of platelet-rich thrombi in small blood vessels, primarily caused by a deficiency in ADAMTS13 metalloprotease responsible for cleaving von Willebrand factor. The classic pentad of TTP consists of thrombocytopenia, microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, neurological symptoms, fever, and renal dysfunction. Not all patients present with the full pentad, and some may have only a subset of these symptoms. [9] HUS is characterized by microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and acute kidney injury, often triggered by an infection with Shiga toxin-producing bacteria (eg, E. coli ).

retrieved_docs.title: Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura Evaluation and Management -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Whether caused by genetics or autoantibodies, TTP is due to the overall decrease in ADAMTS13 (to <10%), thereby allowing its substrate von Willebrand factor to induce clotting. [13] ADAMTS-13 deficiency is the inciting factor, leading to the accumulation of ultra-large vWF complexes with the formation of platelet-rich microthrombi that can embolize and occlude arterioles. The vWF-platelet aggregates are large enough to obstruct microvessels, thus causing clinical and histopathological changes (microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and ischemic organ failure).

retrieved_docs.title: Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) is a type of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia that classically has been characterized by the pentad of fever, thrombocytopenia, hemolytic anemia, renal dysfunction, and neurologic dysfunction. TTP results from either a congenital or acquired decrease/absence of the von Willebrand factor-cleaving protease ADAMTS13 (a disintegrin and metalloproteinase with a thrombospondin type 1 motif member 13). Low levels of ADAMTS13 result in microthrombi formation, which leads to end-organ ischemia and damage. [1] [2] [3] This is due to the inability of the ADAMTS13 to inactivate the large multimer von Willebrand factor (VWF) that is necessary to prevent spontaneous coagulation. Unchecked, the large multimers have a tremendous avidity to bind platelets and initiate thrombi formation. The central nervous system (CNS) and kidneys are the two most common organ systems affected by TTP. Timely diagnosis is very important because TTP is a medical emergency which, without treatment, has a mortality of about 90%. [4] About 80% of patients respond to initial treatment, and the post-treatment mortality is 10 to 15%. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Amegakaryocytic Thrombocytopenia -- Differential Diagnosis -- Acquired Amegakaryocytic Thrombocytopenia
retrieved_docs.content: Consumptive coagulopathies such as disseminated intravascular coagulation, heparin-induced thrombocytopenia with thrombosis, or hemolytic uremic syndrome. [46]

retrieved_docs.title: Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura Evaluation and Management -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) is a rare microangiopathic hemolytic anemia that causes blood clots in small blood vessels. TTP is characterized by fever, hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and renal and neurologic dysfunction. TTP results from either a congenital or acquired absence or decrease of the von Willebrand factor-cleaving protease ADAMTS13 (a disintegrin and metalloproteinase with a thrombospondin type 1 motif member 13). Low levels of ADAMTS13 activity cause microthrombi formation, leading to end-organ ischemia and damage. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Thrombotic Microangiopathy and the Kidney -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Patients should be educated regarding TMA causes, underlying pathophysiology, workup, treatment, and outcomes of TMA to ensure a shared decision-making approach and improve outcomes. As disease recurrence is common, patients should be educated about symptoms indicating recurrent anemia or thrombocytopenia.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS) is a thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA) characterized by thrombocytopenia, microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, and acute kidney injury. HUS is most commonly caused by Shiga toxin (typical HUS) or, less commonly, infections or genetic abnormalities activating the alternative complement pathway (atypical HUS). Additional causes can be secondary to malignancy, autoimmune disorders, genetic mutations, and medication use. [1] Extrarenal manifestations are common in HUS, particularly neurological symptoms. [1] Prompt recognition of HUS's varied etiologies and manifestations is essential for timely diagnosis and intervention, optimizing patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Thrombotic Microangiopathy and the Kidney -- Evaluation -- Laboratory evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Coombs test to rule out immune hemolytic anemia

retrieved_docs.title: Thrombotic Microangiopathy and the Kidney -- Etiology -- Predisposing Conditions
retrieved_docs.content: These forms may need additional diagnostic tests such as genetic predispositions, complement dysregulation, metabolic disease, or other testing to further define their etiology. Overlap with categories above from "Known Etiology" also exists. For example, infection-associated TMA often causes abnormal complement activation, and many patients who develop TMA after a hematopoietic stem cell transplant have a genetic predisposition to abnormal complement regulation. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Amegakaryocytic Thrombocytopenia -- Differential Diagnosis -- Acquired Amegakaryocytic Thrombocytopenia
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B12, copper, and folic acid deficiency;

retrieved_docs.title: Thrombotic Microangiopathy and the Kidney -- Etiology -- Predisposing Conditions
retrieved_docs.content: Infection-associated TMA TMA can be associated with a wide range of infections, including the following: Shiga toxin-producing Escherichia coli, pneumococcal, influenza, human immunodeficiency virus, COVID, dengue virus, or cytomegalovirus infection. Shiga toxin binds to endothelial cells and releases tissue factor and von Willebrand factor, thereby creating a prothrombogenic environment. Pneumococcal and influenza infections are known to cause the production of neuraminidase, which cleaves sialic residues from red cells and platelets and decreases factor H binding, leading to endothelial damage and impaired complement regulation. [20] Other mechanisms of infectious disease-associated TMA can include the activation of platelets, generation of thrombin, or development of ADAMTS13 inhibitors. [21] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis -- Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (TTP)
retrieved_docs.content: TTP is thrombotic microangiopathy characterized by a pentad of hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, renal dysfunction, fever, and neurological dysfunction. TTP is due to a deficiency or mutation in "a disintegrin and metalloproteinase with a thrombospondin type 1 motif member 13" (ADAMTS13) and usually has adult-onset symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: HUS and TTP are subsets within the general category of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MHA). [52] TTP and HUS do overlap in terms of their clinical manifestations. This 'overlap' extends to their manifestation of schistocytosis. Their primary difference is causation. Both diseases can manifest fever, thrombocytopenia, hemolysis, renal injury, and neurologic symptoms. Acute kidney injury in TTP is less frequent as compared to HUS. Whereas TTP is due to the paucity of ADAMTS13, HUS is a complement-mediated illness. The hereditary form requires complement mutation studies to diagnose, while the acquired form requires stool culture and PCR analysis for the Shiga toxin. This toxin is produced by Shigella bacteria and entero-toxin producing E. Coli 0157H7. The patient, usually a child, presents with colitis manifesting bloody diarrhea. The toxin binds to the endothelial walls of blood vessels leading to uncontrolled complement activation, vessel damage, and thrombosis with fibrosis. Blood cells are fractionated as they travel through these vessels leading to their formation as schistocytes. Treatment is supportive, with volume expansion as the mainstay of successful intervention. Antibiotics are of little help.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Thrombotic microangiopathy encompasses various systemic diseases in which endothelial damage causes thrombosis in the microvasculature, including capillaries, arterioles, and venules, resulting in consumptive platelet aggregations. This leads to mechanical shearing of the red blood cells (RBCs), Coombs-negative hemolytic anemia, and end-organ damage. The triad of thrombocytopenia, hemolytic anemia, and ischemic end-organ damage defines thrombotic microangiopathy. [2] [3] Some of the more common TMAs from which HUS must be differentiated are thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP); syndrome of hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes and low platelets (HELLP); and disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC). Despite similar pathogeneses, the treatments of these entities differ significantly. [2] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 713
retrieved_docs.content: The category “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” encompasses a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions, including فقر الدم اللا تنسجي (aplastic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية (hemolytic anemia not caused by autoimmunity), التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). These entities are grouped together because they involve immune system dysregulation, whether through autoantibody‑mediated attacks, cell‑mediated immune injury, or broader immune‑related inflammatory processes. While most of the listed disorders are driven by autoimmune mechanisms, the non‑autoimmune hemolytic anemia is included in the classification for organizational purposes despite its different pathogenesis. Recognizing that these diverse diseases share an underlying immune component helps clinicians identify common diagnostic pathways and consider immunomodulatory therapies across different organ systems, thereby improving patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1800
retrieved_docs.content: Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is identified as a specific subtype of فقر الدم (anemia), indicating that it falls under the broader category of anemia disorders. In clinical practice, فقر الدم (anemia) is frequently associated with متلازمة فانكوني (Fanconi syndrome), a renal tubular disorder that can lead to impaired reabsorption of nutrients and electrolytes, contributing to the development of anemia. Consequently, patients with Fanconi syndrome may present with various forms of anemia, including acute posthemorrhagic anemia when hemorrhagic events occur, highlighting the importance of recognizing the link between renal tubular dysfunction and hematologic abnormalities for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1709
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is classified within the broader category of Other deficiency anemias. The group Other deficiency anemias encompasses several specific anemia types, including فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات (folate‑deficiency anemia), فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات (iron‑deficiency anemia), فقر الدم اللا تنسجي (non‑megaloblastic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), and فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية (autoimmune hemolytic anemia). These specific entities are considered children or sub‑classifications of Other deficiency anemias, illustrating how anemia in chronic kidney disease relates to a spectrum of deficiency‑related and hemolytic anemias and informing clinicians’ diagnostic and therapeutic approaches.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2619
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is classified as a type of Other deficiency anemias. Other deficiency anemias is a broader category that includes several specific anemia subtypes, such as فقر الدم اللا تنسجي (non‑megaloblastic anemia) and فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia). In addition, Other deficiency anymias is related to فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد (cold‑type hemolytic anemia), indicating a clinical or pathophysiological connection between these conditions. Recognizing that anemia in chronic kidney disease falls under the umbrella of other deficiency anemias helps clinicians consider the spectrum of related anemia subtypes—including non‑megaloblastic, acquired hemolytic, and cold‑type hemolytic forms—for comprehensive diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 229
retrieved_docs.content: This “other circulatory disease” category is positioned under the parent disorder اضطراب الأنسجة الضامة (connective‑tissue disorder) and includes the SMQ immune‑disorder group as a member, further connecting connective‑tissue pathology, immune dysregulation, and circulatory complications. Overall, the knowledge network shows that anemia in chronic kidney disease belongs to a heterogeneous deficiency‑anemia family that encompasses both hereditary (e.g., الثلاسيميا, فقر الدم الوبيل) and acquired immune‑mediated hemolytic forms. These anemia subtypes are integrated within the broader immune‑disorder classification (SMQ), which itself is linked to connective‑tissue and circulatory disease categories, illustrating the intertwined nature of hematologic, immunologic, and vascular pathophysiology in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2177
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل), arthritis (التهاب المفاصل), acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب), autoimmune‑mediated hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية), and coronary arteritis (التهاب الشريان التاجي) are all classified as members of the autoimmune disorder group (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية SMQ). These conditions share a common pathophysiology in which the immune system mistakenly attacks self‑tissues, leading to hematologic abnormalities in the various anemias and inflammatory damage in joints and coronary arteries. Recognizing this shared autoimmune basis guides clinicians toward similar diagnostic approaches—such as autoantibody testing—and therapeutic strategies, including immunosuppressive or immunomodulatory treatments, while emphasizing the need to monitor for systemic involvement across both blood‑forming and vascular systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia, non‑regenerative anemia, arthritis, hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions. This grouping reflects the shared underlying involvement of the immune system in the pathogenesis of these diverse diseases, whether through direct autoimmune attack on blood‑forming tissues, inflammatory processes affecting joints, or vascular inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing these entities as members of the immune/autoimmune disorder spectrum helps clinicians consider common immunologic mechanisms, guide diagnostic evaluation, and select therapeutic strategies that target immune dysregulation across these seemingly disparate clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 133
retrieved_docs.content: is noted for lacking an autoimmune mechanism despite its inclusion in the broader immune‑disorder framework. Thalassemia is also associated clinically with sickle‑cell retinopathy (اعتلال الشبكية بالخلايا المنجلية). Collectively, these entities illustrate that various deficiency and hemolytic anemias, especially those within the “other deficiency anemia” group, share immunologic associations and frequently intersect with chronic kidney disease and other immune‑mediated conditions, highlighting the need for coordinated hematologic, immunologic, and nephrologic assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is recognized as an etiological descriptor for several medical conditions, including iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الحديد), transfusion reaction (تفاعل نقل الدم), mortality (التموت), and acute yellow liver congestion (ضُمورُ الكَبِد الأصفر الحادّ). By serving as an allowed etiological qualifier, it helps clinicians attribute these conditions to specific underlying causes, thereby supporting accurate diagnosis and appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 711
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is classified among other deficiency anemias. The category of other deficiency anemias includes folate‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات), pernicious anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل), and iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات). Additionally, other deficiency anemias are related to cold‑type hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد). Recognizing that anemia in chronic kidney disease belongs to this broader group helps clinicians consider overlapping pathophysiological mechanisms and select appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 47
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia associated with chronic kidney disease is placed within the “Other deficiency anemias” group, which also comprises folate‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات), vitamin B12‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل), and iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات). Within this category are several sub‑entities: non‑megaloblastic (aplastic) anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي), acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب), autoimmune hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية), cold‑type hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد), and non‑immune hemolytic anemia (فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية). Although thalassemia (الثلاسيميا) is a hemoglobinopathy rather than a classic deficiency anemia, it is organized under the same “Other deficiency anemias” heading, and sickle‑cell retinopathy (اعتلال الشبكية بالخلايا المنجلية) has been linked to thalassemia, indicating a retinal vascular complication of this hemoglobin disorder. In

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1768
retrieved_docs.content: Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is a specific form of فقر الدم (anemia) that arises rapidly after substantial blood loss, leading to a sudden decrease in circulating red blood cells and hemoglobin levels. As a subtype of anemia, it shares the fundamental characteristic of reduced oxygen‑carrying capacity of the blood, but its acute onset distinguishes it from chronic or nutritional anemias. Prompt recognition and treatment of acute posthemorrhagic anemia are essential in patient care, as rapid restoration of intravascular volume and red cell mass—through fluid resuscitation, blood transfusion, and control of the bleeding source—are required to prevent tissue hypoxia, organ dysfunction, and hemodynamic instability. Understanding that acute posthemorrhagic anemia falls under the broader category of فقر الدم helps clinicians classify the condition, anticipate its pathophysiology, and apply appropriate therapeutic strategies within the overall framework of anemia management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 821
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is classified as an other deficiency anemia. The category of other deficiency anemias includes the conditions فقر الدم الوبيل and فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات, and it also has a subcategory called فقر الدم اللا تنسجي. This classification links anemia in chronic kidney disease to a broader group of deficiency‑related anemias and identifies specific related subtypes within that group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 255
retrieved_docs.content: how “Other deficiency anemias” functions as a central classification node that integrates nutritional, genetic, and hemolytic anemia subtypes while intersecting with autoimmune, metabolic, ophthalmologic, and renal disease domains. Recognizing these hierarchical relationships and clinical linkages supports comprehensive diagnostic evaluation and multidisciplinary management of patients presenting with anemia and its associated systemic manifestations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1489
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia (فقر الدم) is encompassed within the broader category of deficiency and other anemia, indicating that it can arise from nutritional deficiencies as well as other etiologies. Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is a specific subtype that falls under the parent condition of anemia, representing a rapid loss of blood leading to reduced hemoglobin levels. Clinically, anemia is closely associated with the symptom of weakness (الشعور بالضعف), a common manifestation that aids in the recognition and management of the disorder. Understanding that anemia, whether due to deficiency, acute hemorrhage, or other causes, frequently presents with weakness is essential for accurate diagnosis and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: to blood loss, illustrating the interplay between hemorrhagic manifestations and resultant hematologic deficits. Collectively, these relationships demonstrate how hemodialysis can precipitate acute, sudden‑onset disorders and chronic complications, how necrotizing glomerulonephritis represents a severe, systemic glomerular disease, and how renal functional impairment, specific glomerular lesions, autoimmune conditions, drug‑induced reactions, and bleeding‑related anemias intersect within medical classification systems to inform comprehensive patient assessment and coordinated care.
